mappings.rationaleAI: Philosophical inquiry into the existence of God (A.1.a), the extent of human knowledge of the divine (A.1.c), and the problem of evil (A.1.d). Engagement with arguments for and against God's existence connects to theism (D.3.c.i) and agnosticism/atheism (D.3.d). Treatment of miracles addresses D.11, and coverage of science-religion intersection relates to G.2.

Conceptions of God across world religions address the existence (A.1.a) and attributes (A.1.b) of the divine. The question of whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2.a) and singular or plural (A.2.b) is central. Religious experience (B) is explored through various traditions. Arguments for God's existence such as the Five Ways relate to doctrines concerning God (D.3), monotheism (D.3.c), and creation narratives (D.4).

Systematic study of the divine corresponds directly to theology (D), including engagement with sacred scriptures (D.1.a), doctrine and dogma (D.1.b), and doctrines concerning God (D.3). Coverage of salvation relates to D.8, and the study and classification of religions (E) is addressed through comparative theological analysis. Connections to ethics and epistemology reflect religion in relation to other human experience (G).

Fundamental questions about religion's definition, function, and historical change relate to the philosophy of religion (A) and the study and classification of religions (E). Religion's dual capacity regarding contemporary problems such as climate change and armed conflict connects to religion and society (G.3) and religion in relation to other aspects of human experience (G).

Faith's relationship to reason and truth claims addresses human knowledge of the divine (A.1.c) and the philosophy of religion (A). The connection between belief and action relates to religious experience (B). Faith's role in public life, democratic governance, and law connects to religion and society (G.3), and faith's rational justification relates to religion and science (G.2). Faith and morality correspond to G.4.

Free will as a philosophical problem spanning ancient through modern debates directly addresses the special problem of free will identified in A.1.d, examining determinism, compatibilism, and libertarian free will in relation to moral responsibility.

Philosophical analysis of evil as a concept, including the banality of evil thesis and the nature of extreme wrongdoing, addresses the special problem of evil and suffering (A.1.d) within the philosophy of religion framework.

Atheism as affirmative denial of deities directly addresses the existence of the divine (A.1.a) and the category of atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d). The relationship between science and faith connects to religion and science (G.2).

Agnosticism as an epistemological position regarding the existence of God (A.1.a) and the limits of human knowledge of the divine (A.1.c). Philosophically it falls within the category of atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d).

Spirituality as deepest values and meanings by which people live relates to religious experience (B) and mystical theology involving immediate experience of the divine (D.2). Connections to architecture and sacred spaces relate to religion and art (G.1).

Examination of miracle claims across world religions and philosophical definitions of miracles directly addresses the doctrine of miracles (D.11). Engagement with Hume's arguments about natural law and supernatural intervention relates to religion and science (G.2).
Cross-religious examination of angels across Judaism, Christianity, Islam, and Hinduism, including theological speculation by Maimonides, directly addresses angelology (D.5).
The Christian Devil as a supernatural intermediary figure relates to angelology and demonology (D.5). The Devil as embodiment of evil connects to the problem of evil and suffering (A.1.d).
The interdisciplinary relationship between science and religion, including the Galileo affair, Scopes trial, natural theology, and design arguments, directly addresses religion and science (G.2).

Theoretical approaches to myth across anthropology, psychology, and religious studies, including its cosmological and explanatory functions and the distinction from folklore, directly address myth and mythology (F.1).

Comparative world mythology examining universal motifs of deities, creation, flood, trickster, and hero across Greek, Egyptian, Norse, Native American, and other cultures directly addresses myth and mythology (F.1).

Greek and Roman mythology covering the divine pantheon, hero narratives, and foundational myths, with cross-cultural reception from Ptolemaic Egypt to psychoanalysis, relates to myth and mythology (F.1).
Magic as idea and practice across millennia, including its relationship to religion and science and the persistence of magical thinking, directly addresses magic (F.2).
Witchcraft from the ancient world through early modern witch-hunts to contemporary Wicca and modern paganism directly addresses witchcraft (F.3).

Pagan beliefs and practices including polytheism, shamanism (F.4), and sacred traditions encompass several categories within other systems of belief (F). Coverage of non-Christian religions encountered through colonial expansion relates to nature worship (F.8) and ancestor-related practices (F.6).
Superstitious beliefs including luck rituals, omens, and their psychological persistence relate to other systems of belief (F), magic (F.2), and astrology and alchemy (F.5).

Humanism as a worldview without reliance on religion, addressing God's existence and whether morality requires divine foundation, relates to atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d), religion and science (G.2), and religion and morality (G.4).

Fundamentalism as a cross-religious phenomenon with roots in scriptural literalism connects to the study and classification of religions (E). Its political activism and engagement with social issues relates to religion and society (G.3).

Judaism, Christianity, and Islam as interrelated monotheistic traditions address whether the divine is one or more beings (A.2.b) and monotheism (D.3.c). Comparative analysis of three religions relates to the study and classification of religions (E), and the role of state power in religious development connects to religion and society (G.3).
Christian art spanning from medieval devotional works through Renaissance to contemporary interpretations, with liturgical and devotional functions, directly addresses religion and art (G.1).
Ethics as a branch of philosophy addressing fundamental moral questions, including whether morality is objective or constructed, relates to religion and morality (G.4).
Conscience as a moral faculty tracing from Roman through early Christian and Reformation traditions relates to religion and morality (G.4).
The separation of church and state and its philosophical foundations, including blasphemy laws and freedom of belief, relates to religion and society (G.3).
Toleration as a concept for managing religious, cultural, and political difference, including its connections to liberalism and pluralism, relates to religion and society (G.3).
The relationship between religious traditions and armed conflict, including the Crusades, just war theory, and religious pacifism, directly addresses religion and society (G.3).
Blasphemy as a religious, legal, and cultural concept persisting in secular contexts, including colonial blasphemy laws, relates to religion and society (G.3).

The meaning of life as a philosophical and cultural problem engaging with theology, existentialism, and the question of why secularization intensified the question relates to basic questions and problems in the philosophy of religion (A.1) and the broader category (A).

Theory and practice of interpretation spanning textual and religious communication, including the role of hermeneutics in theology and sacred text interpretation, relates to theology in relation to divine revelation (D.1) and sacred scriptures (D.1.a).

Core problems of metaphysics including the nature of objects, causation, and modality provide philosophical foundations tangentially related to basic questions in the philosophy of religion (A.1), though the connection is indirect.

Existentialism's engagement with the death of God, authenticity, and individual freedom relates to basic questions in the philosophy of religion (A.1). Nietzsche's pronouncement of God's death connects to atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d).
Anthropological study of belief systems and comparative religion through fieldwork and ethnography relates to the study and classification of religions (E).

Augustine's thought on free will (A.1.d), the Trinity and monotheism (D.3.c), creation (D.4), and the nature of grace (D.8) addresses several core theological doctrines within the philosophy of religion.

Aquinas's Five Ways arguments for God's existence (A.1.a), synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian doctrine concerning God (D.3), theism (D.3.c.i), and creation theology (D.4) are central to the philosophy of religion.
Spinoza's substance monism identifying God with Nature addresses the existence of the divine (A.1.a), whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2.a), and pantheism (D.3.c.iii).

Anselm's ontological argument addresses the existence of God (A.1.a), libertarian free will theory relates to A.1.d, perfect-being theology to theism (D.3.c.i), and the satisfaction theory of the Atonement to doctrines of salvation (D.8).
Polygamy's intersections with Christianity, Islam, Judaism, and Mormonism, and how marriage practices shape power dynamics, relate to religion and society (G.3).
Covers rites of passage, sacrifice, and ceremonial practices across cultures — directly relevant to religious phenomenology: basic patterns of religious thought and action (unresolved leaf C).
Covers Jewish mystical tradition including esoteric interpretation and Hasidism — directly relevant to religious experience and mystical theology.
